---
title: "Worksheet on Solving Problems by Searching"
subtitle: "Artificial Intelligence for Games — 2nd Semester"
author: "Computer Engineering Department"
institute: "ESTG — IPLeiria"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: darkly
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: false
    highlight-style: github-dark
    smooth-scroll: true
    self-contained: true
---

```{=html}
<style>
  .puzzle-grid {
    display: inline-grid;
    grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 48px);
    grid-template-rows: repeat(3, 48px);
    gap: 3px;
    background: #44475a;
    padding: 4px;
    border-radius: 6px;
  }
  .puzzle-cell {
    display: flex;
    align-items: center;
    justify-content: center;
    width: 48px;
    height: 48px;
    background: #282a36;
    color: #f8f8f2;
    font-size: 1.3em;
    font-weight: bold;
    border-radius: 4px;
  }
  .puzzle-cell.blank {
    background: #6272a4;
  }
  .puzzle-wrapper {
    display: flex;
    gap: 3em;
    align-items: center;
    justify-content: center;
    margin: 1.5em 0;
  }
  .puzzle-label {
    text-align: center;
    margin-top: 0.5em;
    font-size: 0.9em;
    color: #6272a4;
  }
</style>
```

## Overview

In this worksheet we will deal with problem solving using classical search methods — both **non-informed (blind)** and **informed** methods. The goal consists in implementing several search methods and applying them to a specific problem: the **Eight Puzzle**.

The goal in this problem is to find the sequence of actions of the blank space that allow us to reach the **goal state** departing from the **initial state**. Legal actions consist in moving the blank space up, down, to the right, or to the left.

### The Eight Puzzle

```{=html}
<div class="puzzle-wrapper">
  <div>
    <div class="puzzle-grid">
      <div class="puzzle-cell">5</div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell">4</div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell blank"></div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell">6</div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell">1</div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell">8</div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell">7</div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell">3</div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell">2</div>
    </div>
    <div class="puzzle-label">Initial State</div>
  </div>
  <div style="font-size:2em; color:#6272a4;">→</div>
  <div>
    <div class="puzzle-grid">
      <div class="puzzle-cell">1</div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell">2</div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell">3</div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell blank"></div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell blank"></div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell">4</div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell">7</div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell">6</div>
      <div class="puzzle-cell">5</div>
    </div>
    <div class="puzzle-label">Goal State</div>
  </div>
</div>
```

The blank space acts as the sliding mechanism — only the blank can move.

### Project Structure

In the course's Moodle page you will find the scaffold project **`EightPuzzle-Class.zip`** containing the base code. All source files are located in a **single flat directory** (`EightPuzzle/`). The key abstractions are:

| Concept | Class | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Agent** | `Agent`, `EightPuzzleAgent` | Drives the search; holds the problem and chosen method |
| **Problem** | `Problem`, `EightPuzzleProblem` | Initial state, operators, goal test, path cost |
| **State** | `State`, `EightPuzzleState` | Environment representation at a point in time |
| **Action** | `Action`, `EightPuzzleActions` | Operator that transitions between states |
| **Solution** | `Solution` | Output of the search — sequence of actions |
| **Heuristic** | `Heuristic`, `EightPuzzleHeuristics` | Domain knowledge for informed methods |
| **Search** | `GraphSearch`, subclasses | Search algorithm implementations |
| **Node** | `Node`, `NodeLinkedList`, `NodePriorityQueue` | Frontier management |

---

## Part 1 — Analysing the Scaffold Code

### Task A — Core Agent Abstractions

::: {.callout-note}
**Analyse** classes `Agent`, `Solution`, `State`, and `Action`.
:::

Understand how these four classes relate to each other:

- `Agent` — drives the search process; holds a reference to the `Problem` and the chosen search method
- `State` — abstract representation of the environment; must implement `equals()` and `hash()` for use in the explored set
- `Action` — an operator applicable to a state; each action has an associated **cost**
- `Solution` — the sequence of actions returned by the search; includes path cost information

---

### Task B — The `Problem` Class

::: {.callout-note}
**Analyse** the `Problem` class, which describes a generic problem.
:::

A `Problem` has the following attributes:

- **`initialState`** — the starting state
- **`actions`** — the list of available operators
- **`heuristic`** — an instance of class `Heuristic` (used by informed methods; may be `null` for uninformed search)

And the following methods:

| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| `isGoalState(state)` | Verifies if some state is a goal state *(abstract)* |
| `applyAction(state, action)` | Clones the state and applies the action to it, returning the new state |
| `computePathCost(actions)` | Computes the cost of a solution — the sum of costs of each action in the list |

---

### Task C — The `EightPuzzleProblem` Class

::: {.callout-note}
**Analyse** the `EightPuzzleProblem` class, which extends `Problem`.
:::

This class:

- Receives the **initial state** and **goal state** as constructor arguments
- Implements `isGoalState()` by comparing a given state to the stored goal state
- Defines the four legal actions (all with **cost 1**):

| Class | Direction |
|---|---|
| `ActionUp` | ↑ Move blank space upward |
| `ActionDown` | ↓ Move blank space downward |
| `ActionLeft` | ← Move blank space to the left |
| `ActionRight` | → Move blank space to the right |

---

### Task D — The `EightPuzzleState` Class

::: {.callout-note}
**Analyse** the `EightPuzzleState` class, which extends `State`.
:::

This class stores the **3×3 tile matrix** and the **blank space position** (for efficient action applicability checks).

It provides the following methods:

| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| `canMoveUp/Down/Left/Right()` | Check if the blank can move in each direction |
| `moveUp/Down/Left/Right()` | Apply the move, updating the board in place |
| `operator<<` | Textual representation of the state |
| `equals(other)` | Compare two states for equality |
| `hash()` | Return a hash value of the state (required for the explored set) |
| `misplacedTiles(goalState)` | Count tiles not in their goal position (used by heuristic) |
| `clone()` | Return a deep copy of the state |

---

## Part 2 — Uninformed Search Methods

### Task E — Graph Search Algorithm

::: {.callout-note}
**Implement** the `graphSearch()` method in class `GraphSearch`.
:::

This is the **generic graph search algorithm** that all subclasses inherit. The algorithm is described in the code comments:

```
function GRAPH_SEARCH(problem) returns a solution, or failure:
  initialise the frontier with a node for the initial state
  initialise the explored set to be empty
  loop:
    if frontier is empty → return failure
    remove the first node from the frontier
    if the node contains a goal state → return the corresponding solution
    add the node to the explored set
    expand the node, adding successors to the frontier
        only if not already in the frontier or explored set
```

::: {.callout-tip}
The **only difference between search methods** is how `frontier.poll()` selects the next node — determined by the data structure and ordering used for the frontier. This is handled by `addSuccessorsToFrontier()`, which each subclass overrides.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
Use `problem->getSuccessors(node->state)` to expand a node — this applies all valid actions to the current state and returns the list of resulting successor states, each with its generating action already set via `state->getAction()`.

To build a child node, use:
```cpp
double stepCost = state->getAction()->getCost();
auto child = std::make_shared<Node>(state, parent, stepCost);
// or for informed search (g and f explicit):
auto child = std::make_shared<Node>(state, parent, g, f);
```
:::

---

### Task F — Depth-First Search

::: {.callout-note}
**Implement** `graphSearch()` and `addSuccessorsToFrontier()` in class `DepthFirstSearch`.
:::

DFS **overrides** `graphSearch()` to run **without an explored set** — this is what gives DFS its lower space complexity. Without the explored set, DFS only stores nodes on the current path.

`addSuccessorsToFrontier()` treats the frontier as a **LIFO stack** — successors are added to the **front** so the deepest node is always expanded next.

- Uses `NodeLinkedList`; call `addFirst()` to push onto the front
- To avoid the immediate backtrack loop, skip any successor whose state equals the **parent's** state

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Complete** | Only in finite, acyclic spaces (may loop without explored set) |
| **Optimal** | No |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(b^m)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(b \cdot m)$ — **linear**, the key advantage |

---

### Task G — Depth-Limited Search

::: {.callout-note}
**Implement** `addSuccessorsToFrontier()` in class `DepthLimitedSearch`.
:::

`DepthLimitedSearch` extends `DepthFirstSearch` and inherits its `graphSearch()`. The only change is that successors are **not added** if the parent node's depth has already reached `limit`.

- Check `parent->getDepth() >= limit` before adding any successor
- Otherwise identical to DFS's `addSuccessorsToFrontier()`

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Complete** | Yes, if `limit` $\geq d$ (solution depth) |
| **Optimal** | No |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(b^l)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(b \cdot l)$ |

---

### Task H — Iterative Deepening Search

::: {.callout-note}
**Implement** `search()` and `graphSearch()` in class `IterativeDeepeningSearch`.
:::

IDS repeatedly runs a depth-limited search with increasing limits $l = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ until a solution is found.

**`search()`** — the outer loop:

```
limit ← 0
loop:
    result ← graphSearch(problem) at current limit
    if result is not empty → return result
    limit ← limit + 1
```

**`graphSearch()`** — one iteration at the current `limit`:

This class uses an optimisation: the goal test is only applied when `node->depth == limit`. Since all shorter depths were already explored in previous iterations, any solution at a shallower depth would have been found already.

::: {.callout-note}
`IterativeDeepeningSearch` **does not extend** `DepthLimitedSearch`. It extends `DepthFirstSearch` directly and reuses its `addSuccessorsToFrontier()` — successors are added to the front of the frontier without a depth cutoff (the goal-test optimisation handles termination instead).
:::

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Complete** | Yes |
| **Optimal** | Yes (uniform step costs) |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(b^d)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(b \cdot d)$ — linear, like DFS |

After completing Tasks E–H, **test your implementation** with different initial configurations before proceeding to informed search.

---

## Part 3 — Informed Search Methods

Informed methods use a **heuristic function** $h(n)$ to estimate the cost from node $n$ to the goal, guiding the search toward promising regions of the state space.

### Task I — Heuristic: Misplaced Tiles

::: {.callout-note}
**Implement** the `compute()` method in class `HeuristicMisplacedTiles`.
:::

This heuristic counts the number of tiles **not in their goal position**, excluding the blank:

$$h_1(n) = \text{number of tiles } t_i \text{ where } \text{pos}(t_i) \neq \text{goalPos}(t_i)$$

The `EightPuzzleState` class already provides a `misplacedTiles(goalState)` helper — use it.

::: {.callout-note}
The `HeuristicManhattanDistance` heuristic is **already implemented** in `EightPuzzleHeuristics.h`:

$$h_2(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \bigl|row_i - row_i^*\bigr| + \bigl|col_i - col_i^*\bigr|$$

This heuristic **dominates** $h_1$ — it is never smaller and is often strictly larger, leading to fewer node expansions.
:::

---

### Task J — Greedy Best-First Search

::: {.callout-note}
**Implement** `addSuccessorsToFrontier()` in class `GreedyBestFirstSearch`.
:::

Greedy Best-First orders the frontier by the **heuristic value $h(n)$** alone:

$$f(n) = h(n)$$

- Extends `InformedSearch`, which provides a `NodePriorityQueue` frontier and a `heuristic` member
- For each valid successor: compute $h$ via `heuristic->compute(state)`, set $f = h$, create a `Node(state, parent, g, f)` and add it to the frontier
- Skip states already in `explored` or already in the frontier

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Complete** | Yes (finite state spaces) |
| **Optimal** | No |
| **Time & Space** | $O(b^m)$ worst case |

---

### Task K — A\* Search

::: {.callout-note}
**Implement** `addSuccessorsToFrontier()` in class `AStarSearch`.
:::

A\* combines actual path cost with the heuristic estimate:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

- For each successor: $g = \text{parent.g} + \text{stepCost}$, $h = \text{heuristic->compute(state)}$, $f = g + h$
- If the state is **not in the frontier or explored**: add a new node
- If the state is **already in the frontier** with a higher $f$: replace it with the cheaper node

::: {.callout-note}
This implementation does **not assume a consistent heuristic**, so frontier replacement is necessary to guarantee optimality.
:::

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Complete** | Yes |
| **Optimal** | Yes (admissible $h$) |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(b^d)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(b^d)$ |

::: {.callout-tip}
**Why are both heuristics admissible for the Eight Puzzle?**

- $h_1$ (misplaced tiles): each misplaced tile needs *at least* 1 move → never overestimates.
- $h_2$ (Manhattan distance): each tile needs *at least* as many moves as its Manhattan distance → never overestimates (tiles cannot pass through each other).
:::

---

### Task L — Beam Search

::: {.callout-note}
**Implement** `addSuccessorsToFrontier()` in class `BeamSearch`.
:::

Beam Search extends `AStarSearch` and uses the same $f = g + h$ evaluation. The difference is that after adding successors to the frontier, it **truncates the frontier to at most `beamSize` nodes**, keeping only those with the lowest $f$ values.

- Add successors using the same logic as A\*
- After insertion, if `frontier->size() > beamSize`, remove the excess nodes with the worst $f$

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Complete** | No — may discard the path to the solution |
| **Optimal** | No |
| **Memory** | Bounded: $O(\text{beamSize})$ |

---

### Task M — IDA\* Search

::: {.callout-note}
**Implement** `search()`, `graphSearch()`, and `addSuccessorsToFrontier()` in class `IDAStarSearch`.
:::

IDA\* is a hybrid: it applies iterative deepening on the $f$-value threshold rather than depth.

**`search()`** — the outer loop:

```
limit ← h(initial state)
loop:
    newLimit ← ∞
    result ← graphSearch(problem) using current limit
    if result is not empty → return result
    if newLimit == ∞ → return failure   (search space exhausted)
    limit ← newLimit
```

**`graphSearch()`** — one DFS pass under the current `limit`:

- Initialise frontier with the root node (no explored set — DFS style)
- For each node popped: if `node->f > limit`, skip it (update `newLimit = min(newLimit, node->f)`)
- Otherwise: goal test, then expand via `addSuccessorsToFrontier()`

**`addSuccessorsToFrontier()`**:

- Compute $g$, $h$, $f = g + h$ for each successor
- Add to the **front** of the frontier (LIFO stack — DFS order)
- Skip successors that create a cycle (`node->isCycle()`)

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Complete** | Yes (admissible $h$) |
| **Optimal** | Yes (admissible $h$) |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(b^d)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(b \cdot d)$ — DFS-level memory |

---

## Part 4 — Testing & Comparison

After implementing all methods, **test each combination** of search method and heuristic using the configurations in `Main.cpp`:

| Search Method | No heuristic | $h_1$ (Misplaced Tiles) | $h_2$ (Manhattan Distance) |
|---|---|---|---|
| BFS | ✓ | — | — |
| Uniform-Cost | ✓ | — | — |
| Depth-First | ✓ | — | — |
| Depth-Limited | ✓ | — | — |
| IDS | ✓ | — | — |
| Greedy Best-First | — | ✓ | ✓ |
| A\* | — | ✓ | ✓ |
| Beam Search | — | ✓ | ✓ |
| IDA\* | — | ✓ | ✓ |

For each configuration, observe and record:

- **Solution found?** (yes / no / timeout)
- **Solution length** (number of moves)
- **Nodes expanded**
- **Maximum frontier size**
- **Time taken**

::: {.callout-important}
**Expected result:** A\* with Manhattan distance ($h_2$) should expand the fewest nodes among complete methods, demonstrating the value of a strong admissible heuristic.
:::

---

## Summary — Search Method Properties

| Algorithm | Complete | Optimal | Time | Space | Heuristic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| BFS | ✅ | ✅\* | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ | ❌ |
| Uniform-Cost | ✅ | ✅ | $O(b^{C^*/\varepsilon})$ | $O(b^{C^*/\varepsilon})$ | ❌ |
| DFS | ⚠️ | ❌ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b \cdot m)$ | ❌ |
| Depth-Limited | ⚠️ | ❌ | $O(b^l)$ | $O(b \cdot l)$ | ❌ |
| IDS | ✅ | ✅\* | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b \cdot d)$ | ❌ |
| Greedy | ⚠️ | ❌ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^m)$ | ✅ |
| A\* | ✅ | ✅† | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ | ✅ |
| Beam Search | ❌ | ❌ | bounded | $O(W)$ | ✅ |
| IDA\* | ✅ | ✅† | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b \cdot d)$ | ✅ |

\* uniform step costs &nbsp;&nbsp; † admissible heuristic &nbsp;&nbsp; ⚠️ conditional
